refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query and update APIs.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel-grid occupancy model with semantic labels; support incremental updates from streaming sensor data; provide spatial query primitives (collision checks, line-of-sight, nearest-neighbor); expose as a service interface.

DP4: Ingest raw sensor streams and apply rule-based feature extraction (edge detection, clustering, classification heuristics); persist extracted features in a document-oriented store with versioning; provide read-only query endpoints and change-notification subscriptions for consumers.

DP5: Establish a federated feature registry where each feature type (obstacles, hazards, access routes, safety zones) is independently captured, validated against worksite safety policy, and published to a shared model bus; consumers subscribe to feature categories of interest.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

potential_sources: Sensor fusion logic tightly coupled to storage schema—mitigate by defining a sensor-agnostic intermediate representation (e.g., canonical feature format) before persistence.
Query interface design must remain independent of internal graph structure to avoid leaking implementation details to planning/execution modules.
Safety constraint validation should be decoupled from feature acquisition; recommend a separate validation DP or policy-enforcement layer.
Coordinate frame alignment assumptions must be explicit and testable; coupling risk if frame conventions are implicit.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 selected because it provides a single, authoritative spatial model (avoiding duplication across DP2/DP4) while maintaining logical separation between acquisition, representation, and query concerns. The structured spatial graph is solution-neutral (could be implemented as a graph database, octree, or mesh) and supports incremental updates and feature-level queries required by acceptance criteria. DP5 (federated registry) introduces unnecessary complexity for initial scope; DP2 (zone-based) is too coarse for fine-grained planning; DP3 (voxel grid) is memory-intensive and less suitable for discrete feature queries; DP4 (document store) lacks spatial indexing efficiency.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor data (vision, range, inertial) is available and can be synchronized to a common timestamp and coordinate frame. RISK: Sensor calibration drift or asynchronous arrival may degrade fusion quality; recommend periodic re-calibration and buffering strategy.

ASSUMPTION: Feature classification taxonomy is stable and agreed upon by safety/planning teams. RISK: Taxonomy changes mid-project require schema migration; recommend versioning and backward-compatibility in feature representation.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety policy constraints can be expressed as attributes on features (e.g., 'hazard_zone' with exclusion radius). RISK: Complex spatial policies (e.g., dynamic exclusion based on operational state) may not map cleanly to static feature attributes; recommend policy-as-code layer separate from feature model.

ASSUMPTION: Query interface latency and throughput meet planning/execution real-time requirements. RISK: Spatial graph traversal may be slow for large worksites; recommend profiling and indexing strategy (e.g., spatial hashing, R-tree).

ASSUMPTION: Feature updates (e.g., obstacle removal, new hazard detection) are infrequent or can be batched. RISK: High-frequency updates may require eventual-consistency model; recommend explicit consistency guarantees in interface contract.

ASSUMPTION: Downstream planning/execution modules can tolerate model staleness (e.g., features acquired at T0 used for planning at T1). RISK: Dynamic environments may invalidate plans; recommend timestamp metadata and staleness thresholds in query interface.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP1 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Sensor Acquisition & Fusion: multi-modal data ingestion and coordinate-frame alignment (DP1a); (2) Feature Representation: structured spatial graph with geometry, classification, and safety attributes (DP1b); (3) Query & Access: read-only interface with spatial predicates and change notifications (DP1c). This decomposition allows independent evolution of sensor strategies, feature schemas, and consumer APIs. Additionally, introduce a companion DP for Policy Validation: worksite safety plan and operational policies are encoded as constraints and applied to features at query time (or at ingestion, depending on consistency requirements). This decoupling ensures that feature acquisition remains independent of policy evolution and allows multiple policies to be evaluated against the same model. Recommend explicit FR for 'Validate captured features against worksite safety policy' as a separate functional requirement to avoid conflating acquisition and validation logic.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial perception and graph management system that acquires multi-modal sensor data, performs sensor fusion into a unified coordinate frame, maintains a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints), and exposes query interfaces for downstream planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle; raw sensor data acquisition point.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound spatial graph queries and feature data to downstream planning/execution modules; structured spatial feature responses.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety constraint violations or hazard detections derived from spatial graph analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional distributed sensor fusion or remote teleoperation data ingestion; supports multi-agent spatial graph synchronization if needed.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for perception, sensor fusion, and spatial reasoning. Multi-modal sensing acquisition, coordinate frame unification, spatial graph construction, and feature attribute management are core NGHE responsibilities. Query interface for planning/execution is a primary NGHE output contract. Minimal diffusion: only sensor input (Telemetry) and query output (Tasking) cross boundaries; safety constraints are internally managed and exported via SafetyAlert.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform/hardware abstraction layer, not a perception/reasoning module. Placing sensor fusion and spatial graph logic in Vehicle would conflate hardware control with high-level spatial reasoning. Significant diffusion: planning/execution consumers would need to reach into Vehicle to query spatial state; safety constraints would be scattered across Vehicle and Safety Services; coordinate frame management would be tightly coupled to vehicle kinematics, reducing reusability.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop control interface, not a perception/fusion system. Placing spatial graph and sensor fusion in Teleoperation would violate separation of concerns. High diffusion: planning modules would bypass Teleoperation to access spatial data; safety constraints would need to be duplicated or externalized; sensor fusion logic would be entangled with remote command handling.

Weak fit. Safety Services is responsible for constraint enforcement and hazard response, not primary perception and fusion. While safety constraints are part of the spatial graph, the core DP (multi-modal sensing, fusion, unified coordinate frame, structured graph storage) is a perception responsibility, not a safety responsibility. Significant diffusion: sensor acquisition and fusion logic would be orphaned outside Safety Services; planning/execution queries would need to reach into Safety Services; geometry and classification attributes are not safety-specific.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a perception and spatial reasoning capability. NGHE Solution is the designated module for acquiring, fusing, and reasoning about multi-modal sensor data in a unified spatial representation. This module owns the sensor-to-graph pipeline, the coordinate frame transformation logic, the spatial graph data structure, and the query interface that planning and execution consumers depend on. Safety constraints are attributes within the graph, exported to Safety Services via SafetyAlert for enforcement. This containment preserves cohesion (all fusion and graph logic is co-located) and minimizes coupling (external modules interact only via well-defined Telemetry input and Tasking/SafetyAlert outputs).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic perception module, it may accumulate too many sensor types or fusion algorithms, reducing maintainability. Mitigation: Use internal sub-modules or plugins for sensor-specific adapters while keeping the core graph and query logic centralized.

Risk: Spatial graph query performance may degrade if planning/execution consumers issue high-frequency or complex queries. Mitigation: Define query SLAs and implement caching/indexing strategies within NGHE Solution.

Risk: Safety constraints embedded in the spatial graph may not be enforced consistently if Safety Services does not subscribe to SafetyAlert updates. Mitigation: Establish a contract that Safety Services must consume SafetyAlert and validate constraints before execution.

Risk: Multi-agent or distributed scenarios may require spatial graph synchronization across multiple NGHE instances. Mitigation: Use Network Link to support distributed graph consensus or federation patterns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR 'Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model' is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) coverage of agreed site features. The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) exposes a Tasking interface (outbound spatial graph queries and feature data) that directly satisfies the acceptance criterion. Verification can be performed by querying the spatial graph and validating that feature geometry, classification, and safety constraints are present and accessible to downstream consumers.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The allocation from FR to DP to IM is clear and traceable. FR specifies the problem (capture and represent spatial features); DP specifies the solution approach (multi-modal sensing, fusion, unified coordinate frame, structured spatial graph with attributes, query interface); IM allocates ownership to NGHE Solution with explicit input (Telemetry), output (Tasking, SafetyAlert), and optional distributed (Network Link) interfaces. The module boundary is well-defined and responsibilities are unambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP is a complete and logically sound solution to the FR. It addresses all aspects of the functional requirement: (1) multi-modal sensing acquisition (Telemetry input) captures spatial feature data; (2) sensor fusion into a unified coordinate frame ensures consistent spatial representation; (3) structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints) provides machine-readable encoding; (4) query interface (Tasking output) enables downstream planning/execution consumers to access the model. The DP does not over-specify implementation details (e.g., specific fusion algorithms, graph database technology) and remains at the appropriate level of abstraction for design-domain reasoning.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Sensor-specific fusion logic (e.g., LiDAR-camera calibration, radar-IMU integration) may be scattered across multiple internal sub-modules within NGHE Solution if not carefully architected. Mitigation: Establish a clear internal layering (sensor adapters → fusion engine → graph manager) to prevent diffusion of fusion responsibility.

Risk: Safety constraint validation logic may diffuse between NGHE Solution (constraint storage and export via SafetyAlert) and Safety Services (constraint enforcement). If Safety Services does not actively consume SafetyAlert, constraints may be silently ignored. Mitigation: Establish a formal contract requiring Safety Services to subscribe to and validate all SafetyAlert outputs before execution authorization.

Risk: Query interface (Tasking) may accumulate ad-hoc query patterns from multiple planning/execution consumers, leading to performance degradation or interface bloat. Mitigation: Define a canonical query API with versioning and SLA guarantees; implement query optimization (indexing, caching) within NGHE Solution.

Risk: In multi-agent or distributed scenarios, spatial graph synchronization across multiple NGHE instances (via Network Link) may introduce consistency or latency issues if not carefully managed. Mitigation: Define explicit synchronization semantics (eventual consistency, quorum-based, or centralized federation) and document in Network Link contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the natural and appropriate home for this DP. The module owns the complete sensor-to-graph pipeline: multi-modal data acquisition (Telemetry input), fusion into unified coordinate frame, spatial graph construction and maintenance, feature attribute management, and query interface (Tasking output). Safety constraints are stored as graph attributes and exported via SafetyAlert, preserving separation of concerns: NGHE Solution is responsible for representation and detection; Safety Services is responsible for enforcement. The containment score of 0.95 is justified. The only external dependencies are well-defined interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out, optional Network Link), which are appropriate for a perception module. No significant diffusion is observed at the DP-IM boundary.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the internal architecture of NGHE Solution to prevent diffusion of sensor fusion logic. Recommend documenting sub-module boundaries for sensor adapters, fusion engine, and graph manager.

Establish a formal contract between NGHE Solution and Safety Services specifying: (1) SafetyAlert message format and frequency, (2) Safety Services' obligation to consume and validate constraints, (3) escalation procedure if constraints are violated.
Define the Tasking query interface specification (API, query language, response format, SLA) to prevent ad-hoc query accumulation and ensure performance predictability.
For distributed/multi-agent scenarios, document the Network Link synchronization semantics (consistency model, conflict resolution, latency bounds) in the IM boundary specification.
Add acceptance criterion verification plan: specify how the worksite model availability and feature coverage will be tested and validated against the agreed site feature set.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The allocation is clear, traceable, and well-contained within NGHE Solution. However, the gate analysis identifies four critical diffusion risks and recommends internal architectural clarification. Given the high safety criticality of this FR (spatial feature capture directly feeds safety constraint validation), decomposing the DP into next-level sub-FRs is justified to: (1) explicitly address sensor fusion logic containment, (2) formalize the safety constraint export and validation contract, (3) specify the query interface to prevent ad-hoc accumulation, and (4) define synchronization semantics for distributed scenarios. These sub-FRs will drive internal IM design within NGHE Solution and establish clear contracts with downstream consumers (Safety Services, Planning/Execution, Network Link). Stopping here would leave architectural debt; zigzagging down ensures the recommended revisions are captured as formal functional requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 1.1: Acquire and normalize multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) into a unified coordinate frame with timestamp alignment and quality metadata.
FR 1.2: Fuse multi-modal sensor observations into a consistent spatial graph representation with feature geometry, classification, and attribute management.

FR 1.3: Detect, classify, and export safety-critical spatial constraints (hazards, exclusion zones, access restrictions) to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface with guaranteed delivery and validation acknowledgment.

FR 1.4: Provide a canonical, versioned query interface (Tasking) for downstream planning and execution consumers to retrieve spatial features, geometry, and constraints with defined SLA and performance guarantees.
FR 1.5: Synchronize spatial graph state across distributed NGHE instances (if multi-agent deployment) via Network Link with explicit consistency semantics and conflict resolution.

revise.why: No revisions to current-level FR, DP, or IM are required. All gates pass. The recommended revisions from the gate analysis are best addressed by decomposing into sub-FRs rather than revising the parent level, which would risk losing the clear allocation already established.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of spatial feature representation and will not be replicated or diffused across other modules.
Safety Services is a separate, independent module responsible for constraint enforcement and will actively consume SafetyAlert outputs.
Planning and Execution modules are external consumers of the Tasking query interface and will not directly access internal NGHE data structures.
Multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) is available via Telemetry interface with sufficient frequency and quality for real-time spatial graph updates.
Worksite geometry and feature taxonomy are pre-defined and agreed upon before NGHE Solution deployment.
Network Link (if used) is available and reliable for distributed synchronization; single-instance deployment is the baseline scenario.
open_risks: Sensor fusion logic diffusion: LiDAR-camera calibration, radar-IMU integration, and multi-modal registration may scatter across sub-modules if internal architecture is not explicitly layered. Mitigation: FR 1.2 decomposition will enforce fusion engine containment.

Safety constraint validation gap: SafetyAlert may be generated but not consumed by Safety Services if contract is informal. Mitigation: FR 1.3 will formalize the contract with acknowledgment and escalation semantics.

Query interface bloat: Tasking interface may accumulate ad-hoc query patterns from multiple consumers, degrading performance and maintainability. Mitigation: FR 1.4 will define canonical API with versioning and SLA.

Distributed synchronization consistency: Multi-agent scenarios may introduce stale or conflicting spatial graph states if synchronization semantics are not explicit. Mitigation: FR 1.5 will define consistency model and conflict resolution.

High safety criticality: Spatial feature capture errors (e.g., missed hazards, incorrect geometry) directly impact safety-critical planning and execution. Verification and validation must be rigorous. Mitigation: Acceptance criteria must include feature coverage validation against agreed site feature set and safety constraint completeness audit.
evidence_needed: Internal architecture specification for NGHE Solution showing sensor adapter layer, fusion engine, and graph manager sub-modules with clear responsibility boundaries.
Formal contract specification between NGHE Solution and Safety Services: SafetyAlert message format, frequency, validation acknowledgment protocol, and escalation procedure for constraint violations.

Tasking query interface specification: API definition (REST, gRPC, or custom), query language (SQL-like, graph query, or structured), response format, versioning strategy, and SLA (latency, throughput, availability).

Network Link synchronization specification (if multi-agent deployment is planned): consistency model (eventual, strong, causal), conflict resolution strategy, latency bounds, and failure recovery procedure.

Acceptance criterion verification plan: test cases for worksite model generation, feature coverage validation against agreed site feature set, safety constraint completeness audit, and query interface performance benchmarks.
Sensor data quality and availability specification: frequency, latency, accuracy, and failure modes for each sensor modality (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU).
Worksite feature taxonomy and geometry specification: agreed-upon feature classes, attributes, and spatial constraints to be captured and represented.
